evidence: Top-level packages (api, config, exploration, indexer, model, query, utils, viz) each map to a named domain capability; package-info.java files exist in every package explaining ownership boundaries

viz/Edge.java and viz/RelationEdgeType.java are byte-for-byte duplicates of model/exploration/Edge.java and model/exploration/RelationEdgeType.java; viz/package-info.java explicitly says canonical versions live in model/exploration/, yet the copies remain in viz/ unreferenced by any production code

ExplorationService interface in exploration/ cleanly separates public API from implementation; CollocateQueryPort narrows the query surface for handlers that only need BCQL retrieval, documented with explicit rationale in QueryExecutor.java

config/ contains pure loading (GrammarConfigLoader, GrammarConfig) alongside cross-cutting utilities (RelationUtils, RelationPatternUtils, GrammarConfigSerializer) that callers in api/, exploration/, and indexer/ import; this blurs the config boundary from loader to config-utilities hub
WordSketchApiServer wires five focused handler classes; each handler file has a single stated responsibility with package-private visibility -- new engineer can trace route -> handler file cleanly

viz/Edge.java and viz/RelationEdgeType.java are byte-for-byte duplicates of model/exploration/Edge.java and model/exploration/RelationEdgeType.java; the viz/package-info.java explicitly states these types 'live in model/exploration/' but the viz copies remain as dead stale code
No import of viz.Edge or viz.RelationEdgeType exists outside the viz package itself — only RadialPlot (and viz.Edge references viz.RelationEdgeType) — confirming the viz copies are unused

model/exploration/ uses Java records for 7 of 9 data-carrier types (CollocateProfile, CoreCollocate, DiscoveredNoun, Edge, ExplorationOptions, FetchExamplesResult, SingleSeedExplorationOptions); ExplorationResult has documented rationale for being a class; ComparisonResult is a class with factory methods but lacks the same explicit rationale in its Javadoc

Naming conventions are uniform throughout: PascalCase types, camelCase methods/fields, ALL_CAPS constants, *Utils for static utility classes, *Handlers for HTTP handler classes, *Executor/*Port for query interfaces

Package-private visibility is used consistently for internal helpers (CollocateProfileComparator, MultiSeedExplorer, SingleSeedExplorer, BlackLabSnippetParser, CollocateQueryHelper, all api/*Handlers except WordSketchApiServer); public is reserved for the integration surface
HttpApiUtils.wrapWithErrorHandling maps IAE→400, IOException→500, any other Exception→500 and is applied uniformly to every route registered in WordSketchApiServer.registerRoutes()

Exploration layer (SemanticFieldExplorer, SingleSeedExplorer, MultiSeedExplorer, CollocateProfileComparator) consistently uses `throws IOException` plus `throws IllegalArgumentException` for input validation — no silent swallowing

BlackLabQueryExecutor converts BlackLab's RuntimeException subclasses at the index boundary: ErrorOpeningIndex→IOException, InvalidQuery→IllegalArgumentException, bare RuntimeException→IOException — intentional and documented

SketchHandlers.executeRelationQueries catches IOException per-relation, collects it in a `relationErrors` list, and surfaces partial failures in the response as `status: partial` + `warnings: [...]` — deliberate degraded-mode design, not silent swallowing

BlackLabSnippetParser contains 9 `return null` locations for parsing failures (extractLemmaWithFallback, safeParts, extractWordAtPosition, extractLemmaFromXml) rather than Optional — callers null-check inline with `if (parts == null) continue`
RelationConfig (config layer) uses `return null` in deriveDeprel() and deriveSurfacePattern() while other config methods return Optional<RelationType> — mixed null vs Optional in same package

executeCollocations lambda returns `collocateLemma.isEmpty() ? null : collocateLemma` as a sentinel to skip hits — null-as-sentinel inside query execution code, contrasting with the throws-based contract of the surrounding method

GrammarConfigLoader provides two explicit loading methods: load() throws IOException for general callers and loadOrDie() wraps to IllegalStateException for startup code — intentional and documented dual contract

Method names like buildRawCollocateProfiles, fetchSeedCollocates, identifyCoreCollocates, buildCollocateReversePattern, buildNounToCollocatesMap are precise and phase-labelled — a developer immediately understands which algorithmic step each method owns.
Class names SingleSeedExplorer, MultiSeedExplorer, CollocateProfileComparator, SemanticFieldExplorer each name the algorithm variant they implement, not just a generic 'service' or 'handler'.

Identical types RelationEdgeType and Edge exist in both pl.marcinmilkowski.word_sketch.viz and pl.marcinmilkowski.word_sketch.model.exploration — same name, same shape, different package — creating vocabulary duplication and forcing callers to import-qualify or cast.

nounCqlConstraint (SingleSeedExplorer field) vs collocatePattern (CollocateProfileComparator field): both hold CQL pattern strings but use different suffixes ('constraint' vs 'pattern'), diverging from the codebase's prevalent 'pattern' vocabulary.

Variable wordSketchResult is used as a loop variable in MultiSeedExplorer.findCollocateIntersection and buildDiscoveredNouns even though those methods belong to the exploration layer, not the word-sketch layer — bleeds a foreign concept into the variable name.

collocs abbreviation in MultiSeedExplorer.buildDiscoveredNouns (line 141: 'List<...> collocs = seedCollocateMap.getOrDefault(seed, List.of())') is inconsistent with the rest of the codebase, which uses collocates or collocateScores in full.
SEED_COLLOCATE("seed_adj") in RelationEdgeType: the enum constant says 'collocate' (general) but the JSON label says 'seed_adj' (adjective-specific), a mild name/serialization mismatch.

data local variable in MultiSeedExplorer.findCollocateIntersection (SeedCollocateData data = fetchCollocatesPerSeed(...)) is generic — a developer reading the method body must check the type declaration to understand what 'data' holds.
QueryExecutor interface has one implementation (BlackLabQueryExecutor) but documents the rationale explicitly: it isolates external BlackLab API volatility and is used for DI/testability in handlers
ExplorationService interface has one implementation (SemanticFieldExplorer) but enables DI injection in ExplorationHandlers — handlers depend on the interface type, not the concrete class
SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreMultiSeed() validates seeds.size() < 2 and delegates directly to multiSeedExplorer.findCollocateIntersection() — near-pass-through adding only input validation

SemanticFieldExplorer.compareCollocateProfiles() validates seeds.size() < 2 and delegates directly to comparator.compareCollocateProfiles() — the same seeds.size() < 2 guard is also present in CollocateProfileComparator.compareCollocateProfiles(), duplicating the check across both layers

CollocateQueryPort narrow interface genuinely earns its keep: ConcordanceHandlers and CorpusQueryHandlers declare it as their dependency type, keeping their dependency surface to BCQL-only; QueryExecutor extends it cleanly
ExploreResponseAssembler is a tightly cohesive static utility class; all methods serve one purpose (model-to-presentation translation for exploration endpoints), no util dumping ground pattern

HttpApiUtils combines HTTP response writing and query-parameter parsing; documented as an intentional same-concern co-location, package-private, no public API surface — bounded scope limits the abstraction cost

CollocateQueryHelper is a package-private algorithm helper extracted from BlackLabQueryExecutor; well-focused on collocate search/scoring/ranking logic shared across multiple methods of that executor — not a generic util dump
No delegation-heavy classes detected (scanner confirms delegation_heavy_class_count = 0)
No facade/re-export modules detected (facade_module_count = 0)
Wide param bag mentions are false positives: GrammarConfig and RelationConfig are typed records, not raw Map<String,Object> bags

Package-private testing seam exploreByPattern(String, String, String, String, opts) on SemanticFieldExplorer bypasses the RelationConfig abstraction, exposing resolved BCQL strings directly; this is documented as a test-only path but slightly erodes interface honesty of the facade

SemanticFieldExplorer class doc explicitly labels itself a 'thin facade' and documents why the two pass-through methods exist — unusual level of self-awareness but does not eliminate the thin-layer cost
Indirection hotspot chain counts (42-62 in main files) reflect BlackLab fluent API calls (idx.search().find(query).group(...).execute()), not project-introduced indirection chains
pom.xml declares exactly 4 runtime dependency groups: BlackLab (engine + query-parser), Jackson (databind + core), SLF4J/Logback, log4j-to-slf4j bridge — no duplicates for any purpose

Jackson BOM imported via dependencyManagement to align all jackson-* transitive versions; jackson-core also explicitly pinned with an inline comment explaining why ('another transitive dep resolves an older version otherwise')
HTTP layer uses JDK built-in com.sun.net.httpserver instead of Jetty/Undertow/Spring — appropriate for a single-binary research tool, keeps the shaded jar lean
jspecify declared with scope=provided (annotation-only, zero runtime overhead)
log4j-to-slf4j 2.24.3 added specifically to bridge BlackLab's transitive log4j usage through SLF4J — a correct and minimal bridge pattern rather than adding a second logging runtime
All 37 Java source files use only the 4 declared runtime dependency groups; grep over all imports shows no mystery third-party libs

blacklab-github-fallback Maven profile points to a GitHub raw-content URL explicitly labelled 'no SLA' in the pom comment — a fragile last-resort distribution path, though guarded behind a non-default profile
Logging stack (slf4j-api 2.0.7, logback-classic 1.5.18, log4j-to-slf4j 2.24.3) is version-compatible but not managed via a BOM — manual verification required on future upgrades

CollocateQueryHelper.executeBcqlQuery splits work into named phases: collectHits → buildCollocateFrequencyMap → scoreHits; each method has a single named responsibility and the HitRecord record cleanly bundles per-hit state

Guard clauses are used consistently throughout: BlackLabQueryExecutor.executeCollocations, executeDependencyPattern, and SingleSeedExplorer.explore all use early-return null/empty guards before any logic executes

Local private records (CollocateSearch, HitRecord, SeedCollocateData, AggregatedCollocateStats, BcqlRequest, SharedExploreParams, ConcordanceExamplesRequest) are used systematically to type intermediate data without creating public types or field clutter

Helper extraction level is balanced: parseNumericParam<T> in Main.java eliminates real duplication across int/double variants; collectFrequenciesAndPosFromGroups captures a genuinely repeated pattern across three call sites in BlackLabQueryExecutor; parseSeedSet and parseSharedExploreParams extract correctly-scoped concerns in ExplorationHandlers

parseNumericParam(Map, String, T, Function<String,T>, String) in Main.java calls System.exit(1) on parse failure — a side-effect not signalled by the method signature; the caller must read the body to discover this behaviour; handleBlackLabServerCommand already handles System.exit manually for the port parameter, showing inconsistency

scoreHits in CollocateQueryHelper produces two kinds of output in one method: null-collocate hits generate display-only CollocateResult(null lemma, 0 freq, 0.0 logDice) entries intermixed with scored collocate results; the method sorts by logDice descending so null-collocate entries silently sink to the bottom rather than being excluded

aggregateCollocateStats in MultiSeedExplorer builds four separate maps (maxLogDice, totalFreq, logDiceSum, logDiceCount) across a single loop body, then computes avgLogDice in a second loop; a single accumulator record would consolidate this into one coherent data structure with clearer locality

extractCollocateFromXmlByPosition guards position < 1, but every call site pre-checks collocatePos > 0 or collocatePos < 0 before invoking the method; the validation contract is split between caller and callee in a way that requires reading both to understand who owns the check

ExploreResponseAssembler centralises all model-to-JSON translation: collocateProfileToMap, collocateResultToExampleMap, collocateResultToFullMap, edgeToMap, formatSeedCollocates, populateExploreResponse, populateComparisonResponse are all in one class, keeping ExplorationResult and ComparisonResult free of presentation concerns.

ExplorationService is a clean interface boundary: API handlers depend on the interface, not the implementation (SemanticFieldExplorer). The four method signatures exactly mirror the four endpoint operations.

CollocateQueryPort is a narrow port interface for handlers that only need BCQL queries (ConcordanceHandlers, CorpusQueryHandlers), making the dependency surface explicit rather than forcing them to take the full QueryExecutor.
GrammarConfig/GrammarConfigLoader/GrammarConfigSerializer are separated by concern: data carrier, file-IO loader, and JSON serialiser are independent classes with no cross-concern leakage.

wrapWithErrorHandling in HttpApiUtils provides a single, uniform error-to-HTTP-status mapping (IllegalArgumentException->400, RequestEntityTooLargeException->413, IOException->500, Exception->500) applied consistently across all handlers.

Private record request types (ConcordanceExamplesRequest, BcqlRequest, SharedExploreParams) cleanly encapsulate parsed parameter bundles at each handler boundary, making request parsing a typed, testable operation.

buildExploreResponseEnvelope in ExplorationHandlers uses Map<String,Object> for both extraParamFields (goes into the 'parameters' sub-map) and topLevelFields (goes at the root level). The distinction between the two maps cannot be statically verified — a caller could accidentally pass fields in the wrong map.

handleSemanticFieldExamples builds its response envelope with an inline new HashMap<>() and ad-hoc puts, deviating from the buildExploreResponseEnvelope pattern used by the other three exploration handlers. The 'status', 'seed', 'collocate', 'relation', 'bcql', 'top', 'total_results', 'examples' fields are assembled inconsistently with the sibling handlers.

SketchHandlers.handleDependencyRelationQuery is a one-liner that only forwards args to handleRelationQueryForPattern with RelationType.DEP hardcoded — a textbook pass-through wrapper with no added logic

WordSketchApiServer.registerGetHandler and registerPostHandler each consist of a single call to registerHandler with 'GET'/'POST' hardcoded; registerPostHandler doc says 'Mirrors registerGetHandler for POST routes' — pure restatement of a pass-through

SketchHandlers.handleFullSketchForType and handleDependencySketch share identical response-building boilerplate: the 'if (!relationErrors.isEmpty()) { response.put("status","partial"); response.put("warnings",...) } else { response.put("status","ok"); }' block is copy-pasted verbatim in both methods

GrammarConfigSerializer.toJson(GrammarConfig) calls JsonUtils.mapper() once for the root node and then again in the loop body for each relation — the mapper instance is not stored in a local variable, suggesting mechanical copy-paste rather than deliberate reuse

SemanticFieldExplorer's class Javadoc explicitly acknowledges two 'pass-through methods' (exploreMultiSeed and compareCollocateProfiles) that just forward to algorithm classes, though the doc justifies them with a facade rationale
No noisy entry/exit debug logging observed — logging is appropriately targeted (e.g. a single logger.debug for radial center, logger.warn on relation failures)
Domain-specific naming is consistent throughout: lemma, collocate, deprel, RelationConfig, PosGroup — no generic handleData/processItem names found

Documentation on non-trivial methods adds genuine insight: ConlluConverter's inSentence/writer invariant, HttpApiUtils co-location rationale, RelationConfig.deriveDeprel's nullability design note, SemanticFieldExplorer.fetchExamples explaining why executeBcqlQuery is used instead of executeSurfacePattern

viz/Edge.java and viz/RelationEdgeType.java are byte-for-byte duplicates of model/exploration/Edge.java and model/exploration/RelationEdgeType.java — the viz/ copies are never imported by any production or test file (confirmed by grep returning exit 1 for any reference)

viz/package-info.java explicitly acknowledges the migration: 'Domain data carriers (e.g. Edge, RelationEdgeType) live in model/exploration/ so that non-visualization layers can import them without creating an api→viz dependency' — yet the old viz copies were not deleted
WordSketchApiServer.java line 54 contains '// TODO: migrate to Undertow or Jetty if a multi-module build or custom classloader is needed.' — a forward-looking note, not a stale migration artifact
GrammarConfigLoader.validateNoLegacyKeys() actively rejects deprecated 'copulas' config key with a clear error message explaining what to do instead — healthy migration guard
Test comments 'migrated from HandlersTest' appear in ExplorationHandlersTest and SketchHandlersTest, indicating a completed test reorganization with no residual dual-home code
Single JSON library (Jackson 2.17.2) used consistently throughout — no axios+fetch or moment+dayjs style coexistence
No @deprecated or DEPRECATED markers on any production symbol; no shim layer exists in the codebase

viz/ package contains Edge.java and RelationEdgeType.java that are byte-for-byte identical to model/exploration/Edge.java and model/exploration/RelationEdgeType.java — same package-level javadoc, same fields, same enum constants.

No file anywhere in the codebase imports viz.Edge or viz.RelationEdgeType: grep across all .java files finds only 'import pl.marcinmilkowski.word_sketch.viz.RadialPlot' (in VisualizationHandlers and RadialPlotTest). The viz/ copies are dead orphans.
RadialPlot.java itself imports only java.util classes and does not use Edge or RelationEdgeType at all, so the types are not needed in viz/ by any current consumer.

model/exploration/ holds 12 files (out of 59 total production files) — all heavily imported by both exploration/ service classes and api/ handlers. The sub-package cleanly groups exploration domain types away from top-level model (PosGroup, QueryResults).

api/ has 11 files: four focused handler classes (ConcordanceHandlers, CorpusQueryHandlers, ExplorationHandlers, VisualizationHandlers), two assemblers (ExploreResponseAssembler, SketchResponseAssembler), one server wiring class (WordSketchApiServer), one utility (HttpApiUtils), one exception (RequestEntityTooLargeException), and package-info. The 11-file count is at the threshold but concerns are reasonably separated.

config/ package is cohesive: GrammarConfig, GrammarConfigLoader, GrammarConfigSerializer, RelationConfig, RelationType, RelationUtils, RelationPatternUtils — all grammar/relation configuration concerns, no strays.

No coupling-direction failures detected: api/ imports exploration/ and model/exploration/ (correct downstream direction); exploration/ imports model/exploration/ and query/ (correct); no upstream-to-downstream reversals.
No bidirectional directory edges observed across the package tree.

QueryExecutor interface correctly documents @throws IllegalArgumentException for executeSurfacePattern when lemma cannot be extracted; BlackLabQueryExecutor implementation follows this — but the @Override method's own JavaDoc says 'Returns an empty list if the headword lemma cannot be extracted from the pattern' (line 224), which is directly contradicted by the throw at lines 232-237.

HttpApiUtils methods (requireParam, parseIntParam, parseDoubleParam, parseQueryParams) all precisely document and honor their error contracts: IAE on missing/invalid params, RETLE on oversized bodies.

GrammarConfigLoader.load and fromReader correctly declare and propagate IOException / IllegalArgumentException as documented; createDefaultEnglish variants document their unchecked ISE wrapping contract explicitly.

SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreMultiSeed and compareCollocateProfiles both document and enforce the minimum-2-seeds cardinality requirement; ExplorationHandlers adds a parallel guard at the HTTP layer too.

RelationConfig.explorationEnabled field is loaded, serialized in the config API response, and bears a self-documenting boolean name — but no handler or service ever reads it to gate exploration. Callers could rely on this field as an enforcement point when it is purely advisory.

CollocateQueryHelper constructor tension: @NonNull on parameter but @Nullable on field; the test-seam constructor sets the field to null. This is documented in JavaDoc but creates a minor annotation-vs-field-type gap; requireIndex() enforces it at runtime so no silent null escapes.

@NonNull / @Nullable annotations via jspecify are applied consistently across the API surface (QueryExecutor, ExplorationService, HttpApiUtils, RelationPatternUtils) and generally match actual nullability behavior.
buildCollocateReversePattern documents throws IllegalStateException when relationType is absent or POS group has no reverse pattern — both branches throw as advertised.
detokenize in BlackLabSnippetParser is annotated @Nullable return but 'returns text' for both null and empty-string inputs — this honors the @Nullable annotation correctly.

Guard clauses used consistently throughout: null/empty checks at method entry in CollocateQueryHelper, BlackLabSnippetParser, SingleSeedExplorer, BlackLabQueryExecutor — all short-circuit with empty-list or null returns before reaching core logic.

detokenize (BlackLabSnippetParser.java:136-138) is annotated @Nullable (it returns null when text==null), but trimToSentence (line 82-88) and extractPlainTextFromXml (line 123-129) both declare String (non-nullable) return type and pass the result directly without a null guard. The null path is unreachable in practice (callers pass non-null strings), but the contract says null is possible and callers ignore that claim.

seeds.size() < 2 is validated in five separate places: ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti (line 104), ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldComparison (line 149), SemanticFieldExplorer.compareCollocateProfiles (line 199), SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreMultiSeed (line 268), and CollocateProfileComparator.compareCollocateProfiles (line 58). Each layer re-enforces the same contract without a shared helper, making it unclear which layer owns the rule.

CorpusQueryHandlers.parseBcqlRequest resolvedTop logic (line 74-76): treats top=0 the same as 'top absent', silently defaulting to 10. The condition !topNode.isMissingNode() && !topNode.isNull() && topNode.asInt() > 0 is correctly written but the 0-equals-missing behavior is non-obvious with no inline comment.

CollocateQueryHelper.extractCollocateLemma (lines 371-376): the two-branch fallback is clearly sequenced with comments explaining each path — collocatePos<=0 goes straight to extractLastLemma, collocatePos>0 tries positional extraction first. Return null is properly threaded through HitRecord and checked by callers.

JSpecify @NonNull/@Nullable annotations used consistently throughout: ExplorationService interface annotates all parameters and returns; CollocateQueryHelper uses @Nullable on fields and @NonNull on production constructor parameters

Optional<> used correctly for optional fields: GrammarConfig.relation() returns Optional<RelationConfig>, RelationConfig.relationType() returns Optional<RelationType>, SketchHandlers.buildSketch() returns Optional<ExecutedSketch>
RelationType enum is properly used everywhere (RelationType.SURFACE, RelationType.DEP) — no raw string comparisons to enum-defined constants found

BlackLabSnippetParser.detokenize: return annotated @Nullable (correct — returns null when input is null) but the parameter itself is unannotated despite the method explicitly handling null: 'if (text == null || text.isEmpty()) return text'
BlackLabSnippetParser.extractPlainTextFromXml takes 'String xmlSnippet' without @Nullable but guards 'if (xmlSnippet == null ...)'  — null-handling intent is invisible to callers

CollocateQueryPort.executeBcqlQuery(String bcqlPattern, int maxResults): no @NonNull on bcqlPattern; QueryExecutor.executeSurfacePattern(String bcqlPattern, ...) similarly unannotated at the interface level

Systematic Map<String, Object> for all HTTP response envelopes across ExplorationHandlers, ConcordanceHandlers, SketchHandlers — no compile-time shape guarantee. The mix of String, Integer, List<> values is assembled ad-hoc with put(). BcqlQueryResponse in CorpusQueryHandlers is a typed record (improvement), but all exploration/sketch/concordance response envelopes remain untyped
QueryExecutor.executeCollocations correctly annotates @Nullable String lemma and documents 'returns empty list if null' — an honest null contract
GrammarConfigLoader.parseRelationType annotates @Nullable on both parameter and return — consistent with Optional-avoidance pattern for nullable primitives in config loading
RelationPatternUtils.buildFullPattern overloads correctly annotate @Nullable on headword/collocateLemma and document passthrough semantics
SemanticFieldExplorer is a thin facade delegating to three algorithm classes (SingleSeedExplorer, MultiSeedExplorer, CollocateProfileComparator) — responsibilities are clearly separated.
CollocateProfileComparator has a single coherent algorithm split into two named phases (buildRawCollocateProfiles, buildCollocateProfileList) with accurate Javadoc.
ExplorationService interface groups three related exploration modes with clear rationale documented in the Javadoc — intentional bundling, not a god interface.

viz/RelationEdgeType.java is a byte-for-byte duplicate of model/exploration/RelationEdgeType.java. The viz/package-info.java explicitly says the canonical home is model/exploration/, but the viz copy was never deleted.
viz/Edge.java (a record) still imports and uses viz/RelationEdgeType rather than the canonical model/exploration/RelationEdgeType, creating two parallel type hierarchies for the same concept.

No external callers import viz/Edge or viz/RelationEdgeType (confirmed via grep); the api layer and tests exclusively use model/exploration/Edge and model/exploration/RelationEdgeType — so viz/Edge.java and viz/RelationEdgeType.java are effectively dead code whose presence misleads developers about which type to use.
No god-function or excessive parameter list issues found in the seed files or their direct collaborators.
Dependency direction is clean and consistent: api → (exploration, query, config, model); exploration → (query, model, config); query → model; config → model. No violations observed.
ExplorationService interface in exploration package fully decouples api.ExplorationHandlers from SemanticFieldExplorer implementation — api has zero direct dependency on the concrete class.
CollocateQueryPort narrow port is used by ConcordanceHandlers and CorpusQueryHandlers instead of QueryExecutor — explicit, documented boundary discipline.

viz/Edge.java and viz/RelationEdgeType.java are byte-for-byte identical copies of model/exploration/Edge.java and model/exploration/RelationEdgeType.java. No code imports viz.Edge or viz.RelationEdgeType — both viz types are dead duplicates.

viz/package-info.java itself states: 'Domain data carriers (e.g. model/exploration/Edge, model/exploration/RelationEdgeType) live in model/exploration/ so that non-visualization layers (e.g. api/) can import them without creating an api→viz dependency.' The viz package has its own copies anyway, contradicting its own documented boundary contract.
RadialPlot.java (the only production consumer of viz/) uses only RadialPlot.Item (an inner class) — it never references viz.Edge or viz.RelationEdgeType. The duplicates serve no runtime purpose.
api/ExploreResponseAssembler.java imports model.exploration.Edge and model.exploration.RelationEdgeType correctly — confirming the canonical types are the model ones.

JsonUtils.MAPPER is a private static final ObjectMapper computed at class load time (new ObjectMapper()); the Javadoc warns 'Do not mutate the returned instance' but this is a social contract, not a structural guard — any consumer calling JsonUtils.mapper().configure(...) or registering a module would silently corrupt the global state for all other consumers

HttpApiUtils.MAPPER = JsonUtils.mapper() captures a snapshot reference at HttpApiUtils class load time; if JsonUtils were ever changed to vend different instances (e.g. for different serialisation profiles), the captured reference would be stale

System.getProperty("cors.allow.origin", ...) is read at call time (inside setCorsHeader() and warnIfWildcardCors()) rather than at class init time — the comment explicitly documents this as intentional for per-test overrideability; this is exemplary practice

System.getProperty("grammar.config", "grammars/relations.json") in GrammarConfigLoader.createDefaultEnglish() is likewise deferred to method-invocation time, not class initialisation time; no fragile boot-time env snapshot

BlackLabQueryExecutor constructor performs file I/O (opens BlackLab index), but this is called explicitly from Main.handleBlackLabServerCommand — not at class load time; constructor Javadoc explains the deliberate design choice
No static initializer blocks ({ }) found anywhere in production sources

All wiring (executor, grammarConfig, SemanticFieldExplorer, WordSketchApiServer) happens explicitly in Main.handleBlackLabServerCommand via constructor injection; no hidden singleton registries or global state machines
RelationUtils.RELATION_ALIASES = Map.of(...) is a static immutable constant — correct pattern, no mutation risk
StubQueryExecutor shared across all test packages — centralises test-double logic with per-method overrides, avoiding per-test duplication
WordSketchApiServerTest starts a real HTTP server on an ephemeral port (port=0) and exercises the full HTTP→handler→serialisation path with canned data — true cross-module integration coverage

BlackLabIntegrationTest exercises the full pipeline (XML-snippet parsing → collocation accumulation → logDice scoring → result ranking) without a live index via StubCollocateHelper — deterministic CI-compatible integration layer

Live-index tests (BlackLabQueryExecutorTest.LiveIndex, BlackLabBcqlConcordanceTest, BlackLabQueryExecutorIntegrationTest) are guarded with Assumptions.assumeTrue() on CONCEPT_SKETCH_TEST_INDEX env var — no CI fragility from missing external resources
ExplorationHandlersTest covers both happy-path (valid request, 200) and negative-path (missing params, unknown relation, invalid numeric param) tests for every handler method
No snapshot tests whatsoever — 0% snapshot usage across 37 test files
BlackLabConllUIndexerTest is fully @Disabled with javadoc explanation: requires conllu-sentences.blf.yaml not distributed with source — entire indexer pipeline has zero effective automated coverage

GrammarConfigLoaderTest exists in both config and query packages — the query-package copy tests production-grammar content (checking for 'be|appear|seem' in patterns) creating test-production coupling to grammar text

BlackLabQueryExecutorIntegrationTest loop uses Assumptions.assumeTrue(false, ...) inside an IOException catch — IO exceptions from real bugs in pattern processing silently skip that iteration rather than fail
MainTest explicitly documents 'Live index operations are not tested' — Main.java's server startup path (Main.runServer) has no automated coverage at any level

SemanticFieldExplorerInternalTest lives in the exploration package to access package-private overload — this is intentional coupling to internal visibility, will break if method is refactored to private or renamed
All handler methods have a single HttpExchange parameter — the HTTP surface is uniformly thin with no multi-parameter overloads.
SharedExploreParams record centralises the three common exploration parameters (top, min_shared, min_logdice), ensuring all three exploration handlers parse them identically.

CollocateQueryPort / QueryExecutor interface segregation: ConcordanceHandlers and CorpusQueryHandlers depend on the narrower CollocateQueryPort, while SketchHandlers and ExplorationHandlers use the full QueryExecutor and ExplorationService — appropriate and self-documenting.

wrapWithErrorHandling is applied uniformly to every registered route, giving all endpoints the same error contract (IAE→400, IOException→500, RequestEntityTooLarge→413), documented in both HttpApiUtils Javadoc and handler Javadoc.

buildExploreResponseEnvelope is used by handleSemanticFieldExplore, handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti, and handleSemanticFieldComparison for a consistent {status, parameters{relation, top, min_shared, min_logdice, ...}, seed/seeds, ...} envelope — but handleSemanticFieldExamples builds its response map inline, breaking the pattern established by its three sibling handlers.

handleSemanticFieldExamples (ExplorationHandlers) and handleConcordanceExamples (ConcordanceHandlers) return near-identical response shapes — both emit {status, seed, collocate, relation, bcql, top, total_results, examples} — assembled independently in two different handler classes with no shared response builder.

The compare endpoint returns 'relation: "cross_relational"' using the private constant CROSS_RELATIONAL = "cross_relational"; this implementation-internal string leaks as a stable API value with no documentation for clients consuming the field.

seed vs seeds parameter names are clearly differentiated and documented: /explore takes a single seed, /explore-multi and /compare take comma-separated seeds. Cardinality rules are validated and documented in handler Javadoc.

seed and collocate parameter names are used consistently across all four endpoints that take individual concordance pairs (/semantic-field/explore, /semantic-field/examples, /concordance/examples, and the internal helpers).

WordSketchApiServer Javadoc explicitly documents the security model: loopback-only bind (127.0.0.1), all endpoints unauthenticated, with a clear warning that any change to the bind address requires an auth or network-level ACL layer.

All 12 route registrations go through the same registerGetHandler/registerPostHandler → registerHandler funnel, which uniformly enforces method validation and CORS preflight — no sibling routes skip this.

HttpApiUtils.setCorsHeader() is called from every response-writing method (sendJsonResponse, sendError, sendOptionsResponse, sendBinaryResponse), making it impossible for an individual handler to accidentally omit the header.

All handler classes (SketchHandlers, ExplorationHandlers, ConcordanceHandlers, CorpusQueryHandlers, VisualizationHandlers) use HttpApiUtils.requireParam / parseIntParam / parseDoubleParam consistently; wrapWithErrorHandling is applied at the registration site for every route.
warnIfWildcardCors() is called once at startup before any requests are served, ensuring operators are alerted to the CORS wildcard risk before traffic flows.

The POST /api/bcql endpoint accepts arbitrary CQL patterns. It has its own input guardrails (MAX_BCQL_PATTERN_LENGTH=1024, MAX_BCQL_BRACKET_DEPTH=20) but no audit logging or elevated-trust acknowledgement beyond what read-only sketch endpoints receive.

strengths: Every package has a package-info.java that states the package purpose and explicitly rules out what does NOT belong; ownership is documented, not guessed

high_level_elegance.issue_character: Issues are isolated and artifact-level rather than structural: one concrete ownership confusion (duplicate dead types in viz/) and one mild boundary softness (config/ doubling as a relation-utils hub), neither of which materially disrupts the overall decomposition.
high_level_elegance.score_rationale: The codebase sits solidly in the 80-90 range: a new engineer can read the package layout and correctly predict where to find or put things, supported by thorough package-info documentation and well-separated handler classes. The config/ boundary softness (loader + utility hub) is a minor friction point but not misleading. These push the score below 90 but not toward 80.

strengths: Naming conventions are uniform across all packages: PascalCase types, camelCase methods, ALL_CAPS constants, *Utils static helpers, *Handlers HTTP layer — no drift observed

package-info.java files are present in every package and document the package's intent and boundaries, including calling out the correct canonical home for domain types
Java records are used systematically for simple data-carrier types; the one exception (ExplorationResult) includes inline Javadoc explaining why a plain class was chosen instead
convention_outlier.issue_character: The single convention violation is isolated and structural: two type definitions (Edge, RelationEdgeType) that conceptually belong to model.exploration were left as dead copies in viz after a refactor moved the canonical definitions, creating a 'two truths' situation that package-info.java acknowledges but does not resolve.
convention_outlier.score_rationale: The codebase holds its conventions with high discipline across naming, visibility, package structure, and type strategy — a developer can learn the pattern once and trust it everywhere. The one material outlier is the dead-code duplication of Edge and RelationEdgeType in viz, which the package-info.java itself signals are misplaced; this is an isolated leftover from a past refactor rather than systemic drift. The ComparisonResult class-not-record choice is underdocumented compared to ExplorationResult, but follows the same factory pattern and causes minimal confusion. Together these issues produce a small but real 'why is this here?' moment for any developer who opens viz/ expecting visualization-only code and finds domain types — placing this at solid-but-uneven territory (80-83 range) rather than the 90+ strong range.

strengths: Centralized HTTP error boundary (HttpApiUtils.wrapWithErrorHandling) applied uniformly to all 11 routes — IAE→400, IOException→500, any other→500, with no route bypassing it

Exception conversion at the BlackLab boundary is intentional and well-documented: RuntimeException subclasses are caught and re-thrown as checked exceptions so the rest of the stack never sees BlackLab internals

Partial-failure design for sketch queries is explicit: per-relation IOException is caught, collected, and returned as `status: partial` + `warnings` rather than surfaced as a 500 — good resilience for a multi-relation fan-out
Exploration layer uses a single, consistent error contract throughout: throws IOException for I/O failures, IllegalArgumentException for invalid input, no silent catches
error_consistency.issue_character: The core error flow is well-designed from top to bottom, but the query/parsing subsystem uses a recurring null-as-sentinel pattern (9 `return null` in BlackLabSnippetParser, null returns in RelationConfig) that creates implicit null contracts alongside the otherwise explicit throws-based contracts, producing localized inconsistency at the parsing boundary.
error_consistency.score_rationale: The codebase earns credit for its central error boundary, consistent IAE/IOException layering, intentional exception conversion, and documented dual-contract in GrammarConfigLoader — these represent deliberate, coherent design choices that eliminate entire classes of inconsistency. What pulls the score down is that the query/parsing layer breaks the pattern: BlackLabSnippetParser returns null from 9 locations (rather than Optional) and RelationConfig mixes Optional with nullable returns in the same package, forcing callers to use null checks alongside the exception-based contract elsewhere. These are localized but recurring — they represent a consistent pattern of mixed strategy at the parsing boundary rather than isolated one-offs. At 72, the codebase is above the 'significant drag' threshold (60) because the high-level strategy is sound and boundaries are well-managed, but falls short of 80 ('solid but uneven') because the null-as-sentinel pattern in the query layer is pervasive enough to be a real friction point for developers working in that subsystem.

strengths: Algorithm method names label their phase explicitly: buildRawCollocateProfiles, fetchSeedCollocates, identifyCoreCollocates, filterByMinShared — each tells the developer exactly which step of the algorithm it owns.

Class hierarchy names are non-generic: SingleSeedExplorer, MultiSeedExplorer, CollocateProfileComparator all name the specific algorithm variant they implement rather than using vague Handler/Manager/Service suffixes.
Domain vocabulary (collocate, logDice, seed, relation, lemma) is used consistently and precisely across all layers — the ubiquitous language is well-enforced.

Private helpers like prefetchCorpusFrequencies, buildCollocateFrequencyMap, performCollocateSearch are named to distinguish purpose from their callers (executeCollocateSearch vs performCollocateSearch vs executeCollocations).
Constant names like FALLBACK_COLLOCATE_PATTERN and FALLBACK_NOUN_PATTERN are distinguished rather than merged into a single vague DEFAULT_PATTERN.
naming_quality.issue_character: Issues are isolated rather than systemic: one vocabulary-duplication problem with the duplicate Edge/RelationEdgeType types across packages, a few local variable names that are abbreviated or import a foreign concept, and a minor constraint/pattern suffix inconsistency — none of these reflect a global naming philosophy problem.
naming_quality.score_rationale: The codebase demonstrates strong naming discipline: precise method names, non-generic class names, and consistent domain vocabulary make it easy to navigate. The main drag comes from the duplicate RelationEdgeType and Edge types in two packages (same name, different package — a concrete confusion point for any developer adding edges), the nounCqlConstraint vs collocatePattern inconsistency, and wordSketchResult used in exploration loops. These are real but localized defects in an otherwise well-named codebase, placing it solidly in the 'solid but uneven' band rather than exemplary.

strengths: CollocateQueryPort interface segregation genuinely reduces coupling: concordance-only handlers declare the narrower port type, keeping their dependency surface minimal and honest
ExploreResponseAssembler is a cohesive static utility with a single clear responsibility (model-to-presentation translation), not a catch-all helper class

No delegation-heavy classes or facade/re-export modules exist anywhere in the codebase (confirmed by scanner)
abstraction_fitness.issue_character: The issues are isolated and architectural rather than systemic: two thin delegation methods in SemanticFieldExplorer add only duplicated validation that is also present in the delegated-to classes, and a testing seam leaks the concrete BCQL representation back through the RelationConfig abstraction.
abstraction_fitness.score_rationale: The codebase scores well above the 60-point drag threshold: no systemic util dumps, no delegation-heavy classes, no facade modules, and the one-impl interfaces are justified by DI and external API isolation rather than reflexive over-engineering. What pulls the score below 80 is the SemanticFieldExplorer facade, which for two of its four interface methods acts as a thin validation wrapper around a direct delegate call — the same guard check exists in both the facade and the inner algorithm class, and the documented rationale ('one coherent feature surface') is plausible but does not fully justify the duplicated concern. The package-private testing seam that bypasses RelationConfig is a secondary honesty cost. Overall the abstractions earn their keep at a solid-but-uneven level consistent with the 70-75 band.

strengths: No duplicate libraries for any purpose: one JSON library (Jackson), one logging API (SLF4J), one corpus engine (BlackLab), one HTTP layer (JDK built-in)

jspecify correctly scoped as 'provided' so it adds zero runtime or shaded-jar weight
log4j-to-slf4j bridge correctly used to route BlackLab's transitive logging without introducing a second logging implementation
dependency_health.issue_character: The single identifiable issue is a supply-chain fragility (GitHub raw-URL fallback profile for BlackLab) rather than any in-code dependency duplication, version conflict, or heavy-for-light-use problem.
dependency_health.score_rationale: This codebase demonstrates careful, deliberate dependency management: minimal runtime footprint, no duplicates, documented version-pinning rationale, and a lean HTTP strategy. The one mild concern — the BlackLab distribution model requiring a manual install script and a fragile GitHub raw-URL fallback profile — is a distribution gap rather than a code-quality defect, and it is clearly documented. Against global anchors the codebase sits comfortably at 'solid but uneven' trending toward the upper range; the dependency graph itself is exemplary, held back only slightly by the unmanaged logging BOM gap and the supply-chain fallback.

strengths: CollocateQueryHelper.executeBcqlQuery demonstrates textbook phase decomposition: collectHits, buildCollocateFrequencyMap, and scoreHits are distinct named phases with clear single responsibilities; HitRecord binds per-hit data cleanly

Guard clauses used consistently as early returns before logic in query executors (executeCollocations, executeDependencyPattern) and exploration classes (SingleSeedExplorer.explore), keeping happy-path code unindented

low_level_elegance.issue_character: Issues are isolated rather than systemic: a side-effect hidden in a generic utility (System.exit inside parseNumericParam), a dual-mode result method where two concerns coexist without a clear boundary (scoreHits mixing scored and display-only results), and a split validation contract between a callee guard and redundant caller pre-checks. None of these represent a codebase-wide pattern.
low_level_elegance.score_rationale: The codebase sits solidly in the 70-80 range for low-level elegance. Control flow is direct and intention-revealing throughout, state mutation is explicit and local, and helper extraction is consistently balanced with no micro-fragmentation or monolith tendencies — these are genuine strengths that push the score well above 60. What prevents a higher score is a small set of isolated issues that a developer would notice when navigating: System.exit hidden inside a generic parser function breaks the principle of bounded side-effects; scoreHits silently blends two kinds of output in a single sorted list without surfacing the boundary; and the split guard contract in extractCollocateFromXmlByPosition creates unnecessary cognitive load for callers. These are fixable in-place without structural change, but they are concrete clarity failures that distinguish this from an 80-point codebase.

strengths: ExploreResponseAssembler owns all model-to-presentation translation in one place — neither ExplorationResult nor ComparisonResult carry any serialisation logic.
ExplorationService and CollocateQueryPort as typed, narrow interfaces make the handoffs between the API layer and the domain layer explicit and minimal.
GrammarConfig/GrammarConfigLoader/GrammarConfigSerializer partition loading, data, and serialisation into independent classes with no cross-concern leakage.
wrapWithErrorHandling provides a single, uniform error-propagation point across all endpoints — the lifecycle contract at the HTTP boundary is predictable and DRY.
Private record request types (ConcordanceExamplesRequest, BcqlRequest, SharedExploreParams) cleanly type-check parsed inputs at each handler boundary before passing data downstream.
mid_level_elegance.issue_character: The issues are localised asymmetries in the ExplorationHandlers boundary layer: one handler builds its response envelope inline rather than using the shared factory, and the shared factory itself uses untyped maps for structural distinctions that could be expressed with a typed helper.
mid_level_elegance.score_rationale: The codebase scores well above the 60-point 'significant drag' anchor: seams are explicit, translation is centralised, error propagation is uniform, and the port interfaces are well-designed. It falls short of the 85-90 'exemplary' range because the buildExploreResponseEnvelope seam uses untyped Map arguments to convey a structural distinction (top-level vs. parameters sub-map), and handleSemanticFieldExamples diverges from the envelope pattern used by its three siblings — creating a small but real asymmetry that a developer maintaining this handler would have to notice and reconcile. These are contained module-level issues, not subsystem-wide problems, placing the score in the solid-but-uneven 75-80 band.

strengths: Domain-specific naming used consistently everywhere (lemma, collocate, deprel, RelationType, PosGroup) — no generic processItem/handleData/doOperation names in domain code

Documentation adds genuine 'why' context in multiple places: the ConlluConverter inSentence invariant, the SemanticFieldExplorer.fetchExamples design note explaining why executeBcqlQuery is used, and the RelationConfig nullability design note comparing @Nullable vs Optional

Package-info files are concise and informative rather than padded with boilerplate
ai_generated_debt.issue_character: The AI-debt patterns that exist are isolated to the HTTP handler layer and take the form of pass-through wrappers and a copy-pasted response-building block — mechanical repetition rather than a deep structural pattern.
ai_generated_debt.score_rationale: The codebase scores well against most AI-debt indicators: naming, logging discipline, and genuine documentation are consistently strong across all layers. strengths: Single, consolidated JSON library (Jackson) used everywhere — no mixed-library coexistence

incomplete_migration.score_rationale: The codebase is nearly clean from an incomplete-migration perspective: one JSON library, no deprecated-but-called symbols, no compatibility shims, and deliberate enforcement against legacy config keys all push the score up. This does not meet the threshold of 'old+new API coexistence' since only the model.exploration versions are active. strengths: All top-level package names directly reflect domain responsibility: api, config, exploration, indexer, model, query, utils, viz — a new engineer can predict file locations by concern.

package_organization.issue_character: The single structural defect is localized to the viz/ package: it holds two orphaned duplicate types (Edge, RelationEdgeType) that are exact copies of the canonical definitions in model/exploration/ and are imported by no other file, creating a false dual-home for core domain types.
package_organization.score_rationale: The overall package layout is above average: meaningful names, clean ownership, no coupling-direction failures, and correct sub-package decomposition in model/exploration/. @NonNull / @Nullable annotations via jspecify are applied coherently across the main API boundaries and match actual nullability behavior, supporting contract-at-a-glance readability.
contract_coherence.issue_character: The one confirmed contract lie is isolated to a single stale @Override docstring in BlackLabQueryExecutor.executeSurfacePattern that says it returns an empty list but the code throws; a secondary softer issue is the explorationEnabled field whose name implies enforcement but which is never read by any gating logic.
contract_coherence.score_rationale: The codebase is in a strong contractual position overall: interface contracts are reliable, error propagation is well-documented, and cardinality / nullability constraints are consistently enforced. What holds the score back from the 88-95 range is a confirmed docstring lie in the most complex executor method (executeSurfacePattern): the @Override method's own doc says 'returns empty list' but the code throws, a contradiction that could mislead any developer reading the concrete class rather than the interface. The explorationEnabled field is a gentler issue — it exists as a named capability-gating boolean but gates nothing, which could mislead API consumers or future developers. These are two isolated issues in a codebase that otherwise shows deliberate attention to contracts, placing it solidly in the 80-85 range.

strengths: Guard clauses for null/empty inputs are applied consistently across all query, config, and exploration classes, with unambiguous early-return semantics that do not require callers to reason about partial execution.

The SingleSeedExplorer and MultiSeedExplorer both include detailed inline comments justifying their fallback (or no-fallback) strategies — developers can verify that the code path they're reading is the one that was intentionally chosen.
extractCollocateLemma fallback sequencing (positional extraction → lastLemma) is cleanly expressed and each branch is annotated with a one-line rationale, so the logic reads like its intent.
The summaryCounts single-pass switch in ComparisonResult (lines 66-74) correctly avoids three separate stream iterations and the comment makes this explicit.
The performCollocateSearch boolean flag (withStoredHits) is the only shared implementation between two public methods, making the branching logic trivially auditable.
logic_clarity.issue_character: The issues are contract-level rather than behavioral: they arise from a mismatch between what annotations promise (detokenize's @Nullable) and what callers assume (non-null return), and from the same invariant being re-stated verbatim across multiple layers without a shared enforcement point.
logic_clarity.score_rationale: The codebase earns a strong baseline from its consistent guard-clause discipline, well-commented strategy decisions, and correct edge-case handling throughout the exploration and query pipelines. The detokenize @Nullable contract mismatch is a real clarity issue — a developer following the annotation would add a null check that is never needed, or miss the case where it actually matters — but it does not affect runtime behavior. The five-fold seeds.size() validation duplication adds noise without hiding bugs. These issues are 'solid but uneven' in the 80-range sense: the main body of logic is clear and provably correct, but two pattern-level inconsistencies prevent a higher score.

strengths: JSpecify @NonNull/@Nullable annotations applied consistently on public API interfaces (ExplorationService, QueryExecutor, RelationPatternUtils) and cross-module boundaries
Optional<> used correctly for fields that are genuinely optional (RelationType, RelationConfig lookup) rather than overused as nullable wrappers

type_safety.issue_character: The issues are not deep structural breaks but a consistent annotation-discipline gap in two areas: (1) method parameters that handle null in the body but are not annotated @Nullable, missing the contract signal for callers; and (2) a systematic `Map<String, Object>` pattern for HTTP response envelopes throughout the API layer, where typed records would provide compile-time shape guarantees.
type_safety.score_rationale: The codebase sits solidly in the 65–75 range. On the positive side, the JSpecify adoption is genuine and covers the most important cross-module interfaces; Optional is used with restraint and correctness; enums are not bypassed. What pulls the score down is the systematic Map<String, Object> for response assembly — present in four handler classes and ExploreResponseAssembler — which makes response shapes invisible to the compiler, and a scattered annotation gap where null-handling methods omit @Nullable on their parameters. These are not latent bugs but they reduce the value of the type system as a correctness aid. The code is closer to 'solid but uneven' (80 anchor) than 'significant drag' (60 anchor), and the untyped response pattern is a recurring subsystem-wide issue that keeps it below 75.

strengths: Responsibilities decomposed into focused algorithm classes (SingleSeedExplorer, MultiSeedExplorer, CollocateProfileComparator) rather than one monolithic explorer — each class has a single coherent job.

ExplorationService interface groups methods that share corpus infrastructure without mixing in unrelated concerns.

design_coherence.issue_character: The single structural issue is an incomplete migration: a canonical type (RelationEdgeType and its companion Edge) was moved to model/exploration/ but the old viz/ copies were never deleted, leaving two definitions of the same concept in the codebase.
design_coherence.score_rationale: The exploration module is well-decomposed — focused classes, earned abstractions, no parameter sprawl. strengths: Dependency direction is uniformly top-down (api → exploration/query → model/config) with no cycles or reverse dependencies observed across the entire codebase.

viz/package-info.java explicitly documents the intended boundary (canonical types live in model/exploration) — the architecture intent is recorded, even if execution is incomplete.
cross_module_architecture.issue_character: A single, self-acknowledged boundary drift: viz.Edge and viz.RelationEdgeType are orphaned identical copies of model.exploration types that the viz package-info explicitly says belong in model/exploration; the drift is contained to one package but contradicts the documented contract.
cross_module_architecture.score_rationale: The layering, interface abstractions, and narrow-port discipline are all solid — this codebase is clearly above the 'significant drag' threshold. The orphaned duplicates in viz are the one concrete boundary violation: they are dead code, their own package-info refutes them, and they represent a documented architecture contract that was not fully carried through. This is a single module-scoped issue rather than a systemic pattern, so it pulls the score modestly below 'solid but uneven (80)' — landing around 73 where the architecture is largely trustworthy but has one unresolved residue that a developer maintaining viz would eventually hit.

strengths: System property reads for 'cors.allow.origin' and 'grammar.config' are deferred to call time rather than class initialisation, documented with explicit rationale — test-overrideable by design

authorization_consistency.issue_character: The single gap is the /api/bcql endpoint, which offers a materially more powerful capability (arbitrary corpus queries) than read-only sketch endpoints yet receives identical trust treatment and no audit logging — an asymmetry that is benign under the current loopback-only model but would become a real inconsistency if the server were exposed externally.
authorization_consistency.score_rationale: The codebase applies a coherent, explicitly documented security model uniformly across all routes with no observable inconsistency: every route is unauthenticated for the same stated reason (loopback-only bind), every handler validates inputs the same way, and CORS is managed centrally. This places it comfortably above the 'significant drag' threshold. The only gap keeping the score from the 90+ range is the /api/bcql endpoint's elevated power (arbitrary queries) without any form of audit log or differentiated access note, which — while acceptable under the current deployment model — is a latent inconsistency waiting to matter if the bind address ever changes.

evidence: @Disabled annotation on class with comment: 'Requires conllu-sentences.blf.yaml format config in the project root — not distributed with source'
The test class has 5 well-written tests (token count, doc count, directory creation, missing-input IOException, zero-token-count before indexing) but none ever runs in CI
ConlluConverter tests do run, but the indexer integration — which calls DocumentFormats.addConfigFormatsInDirectories — is untested

issues.summary: GrammarConfigLoaderTest duplicated in two packages — query-package copy couples tests to production grammar text strings
issues.suggestion: Remove query/GrammarConfigLoaderTest entirely — its grammar-content assertions belong in RelationsValidationTest (which already checks all relations for non-null patterns and valid positions). If copula-lemma content must be verified, add it to RelationsValidationTest with a comment explaining the contract.
evidence: query/GrammarConfigLoaderTest asserts pattern.contains("be|appear|seem") — hardcoded production grammar text; if copula list changes the test breaks for unrelated reasons
config/GrammarConfigLoaderTest tests GrammarConfigLoader.fromReader with inline JSON — correctly scoped to loading logic
Both classes use the same @DisplayName style and test the same GrammarConfigLoader class from different angles, with overlapping assertions

issues.summary: BlackLabQueryExecutorIntegrationTest swallows IOExceptions with Assumptions.assumeTrue(false) — real bugs in pattern processing silently skip rather than fail
issues.suggestion: Catch only the specific BlackLab exception type for unsupported pattern (e.g., InvalidQuery or a BlackLab-specific subclass), and let IOException propagate as a test failure. If multiple index variants are expected, parameterise the test or document which patterns are known-unsupported with explicit assumeTrue checks.
evidence: catch (IOException e) { Assumptions.assumeTrue(false, "Test skipped: index does not support pattern '" + p + "': " + e.getMessage()); } — any IOException including programming errors causes the loop iteration to be silently skipped
The intent is to handle patterns unsupported by a particular index variant, but the broad IOException catch means misconfigured patterns or code bugs are also silently ignored
This pattern appears inside a for-loop over 6 patterns — a partial failure (1 of 6 patterns throwing) produces a green test

issues.summary: handleSemanticFieldExamples builds response inline instead of using buildExploreResponseEnvelope like its three sibling exploration handlers
issues.suggestion: Extract a lightweight examples-response builder method (or extend buildExploreResponseEnvelope) and use it in handleSemanticFieldExamples so the examples endpoint's envelope matches the format of the other exploration endpoints.
evidence: handleSemanticFieldExplore, handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti, and handleSemanticFieldComparison all call buildExploreResponseEnvelope to produce {status, parameters{...}, seed/seeds, ...}.

handleSemanticFieldExamples at line ~195 constructs its own Map<String,Object> with response.put("status","ok"), response.put("seed",...), etc. — bypassing the shared envelope builder and producing a response without a 'parameters' sub-object.

issues.summary: handleSemanticFieldExamples and handleConcordanceExamples produce near-identical response maps with no shared response builder
issues.suggestion: Move the shared response-map construction into ExploreResponseAssembler as a static buildExamplesResponse(seed, collocate, relation, bcql, top, results, fallback) method so both handlers delegate to a single authoritative shape definition.
evidence: ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldExamples lines ~195-202: response.put("status","ok"), put("seed"), put("collocate"), put("relation"), put("bcql"), put("top"), put("total_results"), put("examples").
ConcordanceHandlers.handleConcordanceExamples lines ~69-80: identical key set with the same put order, plus a conditional put("fallback",true).
Both call ExploreResponseAssembler::collocateResultToExampleMap for the examples list.

issues.summary: The compare endpoint emits 'relation: "cross_relational"' using a private Java constant with no client-facing documentation of this value
issues.suggestion: Promote CROSS_RELATIONAL to a well-typed enum value or a public constant with a Javadoc note, and add a comment in handleSemanticFieldComparison explaining that cross_relational is the canonical sentinel value clients should expect and handle.
evidence: private static final String CROSS_RELATIONAL = "cross_relational"; — defined as a package-private string constant.
buildExploreResponseEnvelope(CROSS_RELATIONAL, ...) puts this string as the "relation" field value in the compare endpoint's response body.
No Javadoc, package-info, or API comment explains to clients that the compare endpoint will always return 'relation: "cross_relational"' as a sentinel.

issues.summary: POST /api/bcql accepts arbitrary corpus queries with no audit logging, unlike all read-only sibling endpoints
issues.suggestion: Add at least an INFO-level log entry in handleCorpusQuery that records the caller's query string (already sanitized by the length/bracket guards), so that if the server is later re-deployed with a wider bind address, a minimal audit trail exists. Optionally, add a comment in registerRoutes marking /api/bcql as a write-capable or elevated-risk endpoint so a future auth layer knows to treat it differently from read-only routes.
evidence: CorpusQueryHandlers.handleCorpusQuery logs only at DEBUG level ('BCQL query: {}') — no INFO/WARN-level audit trail distinguishing it from routine read queries.

All other endpoints (sketch, explore, concordance) also log at DEBUG; however those endpoints execute bounded, schema-defined queries while /api/bcql executes caller-supplied CQL patterns up to 1024 chars.

WordSketchApiServer Javadoc notes that external exposure requires 'an authentication or network-level access-control layer' but makes no specific mention of /api/bcql being higher-risk within that future context.
